definitions: A custard-like dessert made with egg yolks, sugar and Marsala wine.

[…] "Brainy" didn't accept, didn't refuse, wasn't offended, didn't seem to understand. He told her that she had better talk to her instructor about getting tutoring and a re-exam. Now Miss Zaftig is circulating the story that Prof "No Brain" must be a eunuch or a robot. Not even a homo. Totally sexless.

definitions: One of a series of sharp turns or reversals.
examples: Afterward, she returns to the Dragon Pit to announce her agreement to the truce. “The darkness is coming for us all. We will face it together. And when the Great War is over, perhaps you will remember that I chose to help, with no promises or assurances from any of you. I expect not.” The self-pity and pettiness is classic Cersei, but what a zag! It was always hard to imagine what this show looks like with all the humans on all sides temporarily working together.

definitions: To move with a sharp turn or reversal.
(Islam) Almsgiving, usually in the form of an annual tax on certain types of property which is then used for charitable purposes; the third of the five pillars of Islam.
examples: Services given by the Sayyids are supposed to be free, but they should receive the Zikat, or one tenth of the fruits of the earth.

The Alcoran and all the subsequent teachings of the faithful enjoined a constant and liberal charity. […] The theory of the zakat, or tax, is that it is a voluntary gift. Contributing for the needs of the state followed in due course of time, and became obligatory. These taxes, or zakat, amounted by law to two and a half per cent. of the principal of certain assessed property.

Amongst these stations is the well-known place called Qaṭyā, which the people pronounce Qaṭya, where zakāt is collected from the merchants, their goods are examined, and their baggage most rigorously searched.

The Mahometan law insists on the legal obligation of society to support their indigent fellow-citizens, the Hedaya or Mahometan Guide begins, "take zakat," said Mahomet, "from the rich Mussulmans, and bestow it upon the poor Mussulmans: zakat or charity," says the code, "signifies alms imposed by law, in opposition to [sadka], voluntary charity." […] The zakat was a property-tax paid on all productions and profits, on merchandise, mines, &c.

Zakât is an ordinance of God, incumbent upon every perſon vvho is free, ſane, adult, and a Muſſulman, provided he be poſſeſſed, in full propriety, of ſuch eſtate or effects as are termed in the language of the lavv a Niſâb, and that he has been in poſſeſſion of the ſame for the ſpace of one complete year, vvhich is denominated Havvlân-Hâvvl. The reaſon of this obligation is found in the vvord of God, vvho has ordained it in the Koran, ſaying, " Bestovv Zakât."

definitions: Albizia saman, a large tropical tree in the pea family.
examples: On shore, the mule's pinkish ears revolved lazily in the shade of the zaman tree.

definitions: Any of various cycads of the genera Zamia and Macrozamia

examples: Then the computer went zap and I lost all my work.

examples: For Modes of Faith let graceleſs zealots fight; / His can't be wrong whoſe life is in the right:

examples: Doubtless many will exclaim against the Roman Catholic Church for this; but the simple truth is that Protestantism was no less zealous against the new scientific doctrine.

definitions: (astronomy) The highest point in the sky reached by a celestial body.

definitions: Alternative letter-case form of Zeppelin.
The numeric symbol that represents the cardinal number zero.
(informal, uncountable) Nothing, or none.
The digit 0 in the decimal, binary, and all other base numbering systems.
The point on a scale at which numbering or measurement originates.
(slang) A person dismissed as unimportant.
(finance) A security which has a zero coupon (paying no periodic interest).
examples: The zeroes of a polynomial are its roots by the fundamental theorem of algebra.
The quotient ring over a maximal ideal is a field with a single zero element.
The takeovers were financed by issuing zeroes.
The derivative of a continuous, differentiable function that twice crosses the axis must have a zero.
Since a commutative zero is the inverse of any additive identity, it must be unique when it exists.

definitions: To disappear or make something disappear.
To adjust until the variance is reduced to an acceptably low amount.
To set some amount to be zero.
examples: George parked in space 34, zeroed the trip meter, closed and locked his car, then went back to the guard shack.
The soldier took his gun to the shooting range to zero its aim.
They tried to zero the budget by the end of the quarter.

definitions: (meteorology) Of horizontal visibility, limited to 165 feet (50.3 meters) or less.
Used in the names of foodstuffs, especially beverages, to indicate a version with no calories

examples: The stem of "kobieta" with the zero ending is "kobiet".

definitions: The cardinal number occurring before one and that denotes no quantity or amount at all, represented in Arabic numerals as 0.
examples: A cheque for zero dollars and zero cents crashed the computers on division by zero.
The conductor waited until the passenger count was zero.

definitions: Corresponding to a position preceding the first.
(mathematics) In the initial position in a sequence whose elements are numbered starting at zero; the ordinal number corresponding to zero.
examples: A and C are in equilibrium following the Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics.

definitions: (computing, informal) a zebibit.

definitions: (rhetoric) Syllepsis.
(rhetoric) The act of using a word, particularly an adjective or verb, to apply to more than one noun when its sense is appropriate to only one.
examples: Thus I would describe 'He took his leave and the wrong umbrella' as zeugma, but not 'He took his hat and umbrella'. Zeugma is an important element in Chaucer's poetic technique, not because he uses it, but because he so regularly turns it down.

definitions: (Chinese contexts) Synonym of congee, a thick rice porridge.
examples: We did, however, send them to a Chinese nursery school that fed them a daily lunch of zhou, a rice porridge with various seasonings: pickled turnips, flakes of dough sticks, green or red beans, sesame paste, or something called hot prickly mustard tubers.

examples: As for daytime sun protection, Dr. Bowe said mineral sunscreens made with zinc oxide may be gentler on sensitized skin than chemical formulas.

Even if there were a Chef and Brewer near Marble Arch, the name suggests a pint and pie, not a prawn and tomato sandwich and “a dock glass of white port off the zinc ”; i.e., a small glass of a fine aperitif, at the counter […]

definitions: A dry red wine of California.
A small black grape from which zinfandel wine is made.
examples: This zinfandel ’s delish. Where’d you get it?

definitions: Zest or vitality.
(slang) A witty insult or derogatory remark.
A short high-pitched humming sound, such as that made by a bullet or vibrating string.
(uncountable, slang) Pleasant or exciting flavour of food.
examples: I heard a zing close to my head and looked up again. Five or six men were lined up on the deck above me with rifles shooting at the shark.
To accompany a meal, the fresh Italian lager Poretti is perfect; Erdinger Weisbier, a wheat beer from Germany, is full of zing;

definitions: (transitive, slang) To address a witty insult or comeback to.
To move very quickly, especially while making a high-pitched hum.
examples: We are all a second or two older than an astronaut who has been zinging around the Earth at 18000 miles per hour, because of his or her greater speed and the lack of gravity.
Goomba: Ouch that was cold! Paragoomba: “What, you’re too good for us? Come off it, sister! Spike Goomba: “Nobody zings us like that! Nobody! Let’s get ’em!”

When the caller identified herself as from the Fred Hutchinson Cancer Research Center in Seattle my heart zinged. I heard the words we had yearned for since March: They had found a donor marrow, mismatched at one antigen, that might work for Jane.

definitions: (onomatopoeia) A high pitched humming sound.
(US, slang) Used to acknowledge a witty comeback, a zinger.
examples: Something inside of me started a symphony / Zing! Went the strings of my heart
Zing! The joke had rebounded back on the one who started it. "Ouch!" Mama Jane winced ruefully. We all dissolved in laughter at the good-natured repartee.

definitions: (informal) A very rapidly moving object, especially one that is thrown.
(informal) An outstanding, energetic and surprising thing or person.
(informal) A surprising or unusually pointed, humorous and impressive insult or insulting quip.
A piece of food or drink that is tart and stimulating.
(informal) An event that when experienced leaves the witness dazed, either physically or metaphorically.
examples: I was still reeling from the zinger of seeing my ex on a date with my best friend.
The pitcher threw a real zinger and struck him out.
Here, have a strawberry zinger and don't tell Mom and Dad that we had candy in the bedroom.
Think about all the scientists—and all the microscopes and lab equipment and testing—that may come up with zero instead of a zinger.
You might think it's almost worthwhile calling them and restarting the argument just to get that final zinger in. Why do you think of something good to say when it's too late?

definitions: Any member of the genus Zingiber of gingers.
(mineralogy) A grey mineral with hexagonal crystals, Pb 9 Sb 22 S 42.
Any of several brightly coloured flowering plants, of the genus Zinnia, native to tropical America; old maid.
examples: Santa sighed at the unfairness of it all and slammed the picture down on the mantelpiece among the bowl of wax fruit and the bouquet of paper zinnias and the statue of the Virgin Mary and the figurine of the Infant of Prague.

[…] the Consul at this moment greeted Mr. Quincey's cat, momentarily forgetting its owner again as the grey, meditative animal, with a tail so long it trailed on the ground, came stalking through the zinnias: […]

definitions: (mineralogy) A mineral with monoclinic crystals, K Li Fe Al (Al Si 3) O 10 (F,O H) 2, of the mica group.
zayin, Hebrew letter ז.
Alternative letter-case form of Zionist.
(computing, informal) A zip file.
(slang) Zero; nothing.
(UK, Ireland, Commonwealth) A zip fastener.
The high-pitched sound of a small object moving rapidly through air.
A trip on a zipline.
Ellipsis of zip code.
(informal) Energy; vigor; vim.
examples: I know zip about economics.

definitions: To make (something) move quickly
To close with a zip fastener
(intransitive, followed by a preposition) To move rapidly (in a specified direction or to a specified place) with a high-pitched sound.
(transitive, computing) To compress (one or more computer files) into a single and often smaller file, especially one in the ZIP format.
(intransitive, colloquial, followed by a preposition) To move in haste (in a specified direction or to a specified place).
(transitive, figuratively) To close as if with a zip fastener. (For example, 'She zipped up her coat and left.')
examples: The bullet zipped through the air.
Zip down to the shops for some milk.

definitions: (onomatopoeia) Imitative of high-pitched sound of a small object moving rapidly through air.
examples: ` Zip!' Just like that, an exquisitely shaped fragment of a flashing rainbow in flight flits across in front of us[.]

definitions: (biochemistry) A leucine zipper.
(slang) A scar on a person's body.
(BDSM) A string of clothes pegs or clips attached to the body and then quickly pulled off.
A zipline.
(chiefly US, Australia) A zip fastener.
(programming) A technique for arbitrarily traversing an aggregate data structure and updating its contents. See zipper (data structure).
A pressure -sensitive plastic closure, as on a Ziploc bag.
examples: He got his T-shirt stuck in the zipper of his jacket.
I also competed in track and field at Idaho, and it's all because of putting the shot that I've got my long scar, my zipper, down the back of my neck.

Skills acquired include learning the "Flea Hop" and the "Tarzan Swing" as well as "Riding the Zipper " (sliding 400 feet on a cable between two mountain peaks), canoeing in white-water rapids, bushwacking and learning to "rappel" hundreds of feet down sheer rock cliffs on a rope.

They would handle all the night combat air patrols, the night hecklers who would go over the enemy airfields, and we formed a new little gimmick called " zippers " to help close that gap between the time the daylight strikes left the target and darkness.

[…] the usual gap between the time the last strike of the day departed and the first night hecklers arrived was closed with " zippers " — night fighters who, using day fighter tactics, proceeded to the target in daylight in time to relieve the last day blanket patrol.

definitions: To close a zipper.
To put a zipper on an article.
To act in a manner similar to a zipper.
examples: He zippered his sweater against the cold.
These fall jackets are zippered.
If more drivers knew how to zipper, the accident would not have disrupted traffic so much.

definitions: (informal, emphatic) Zero. Typically used for emphasis, as when the listener might expect a positive quantity.
examples: We got 200 consoles and we sold 200 consoles and 200 minus 200 is ZIPPO, so I can't sell you anything right now.

definitions: (slang, transitive) To light on fire.
examples: The youth climbed off, Zippoed a wick in a glass bottle and walked towards Jake's flat.
I didn't want to burn 'em out, but that's policy. So we zippoed their hooch.
Once the last villagers had left Ben Suc, the buildings were doused with petrol and zippoed.

definitions: (slang) Zero; none whatsoever.
examples: “The whole situation is magnificent news, especially when the problem has been zippo investment by large corporations in Africa,” says Robert H. Bates […].

definitions: (informal) Quick, speedy.
(informal) Energetic and lively.
examples: [T]he rising wind, cool and zippy from the far ice floes of Bering Straits— [...]
Fine department stores showing the zippiest of embroidered gloves imported from Scandinavia and the fluffiest of knitted dresses imported from Wisconsin.
Fun is the doughboy's pal—that's why he wrote and sings " K-K-K-Katy "—the song of songs, with a zippy, catchy melody and those beautifully simple words stammered by Katy's tongue-tied beau.

He had pored over the websites of the different European car manufacturers, wooed at first by the Beemers, Audis and Saabs before admitting we had a restricted budget. He eventually chose an economical but zippy Renault hatchback.
Apart from anything else, the zippier Northern cadences meant the radio stations could pack in three or four commercials in the time it would take the average Southerner to clear his throat.

definitions: A crystal of zircon, sometimes used as a false gemstone.
A mineral occurring in tetragonal crystals, usually of a brown or grey colour and consisting of silica and zirconia.
examples: A zircon princess, seemed to lost her senses

Although there are dozens of different types of gems, among the best known and most important are […]. (Common gem materials not addressed in this article include amber, amethyst, chalcedony, garnet, lazurite, malachite, opals, peridot, rhodonite, spinel, tourmaline, turquoise and zircon.)

[…] salamanders, mermaids, chimaeras, wild men o’ the woods, leviathans, all hewn from faultless gems, thrice the bulk of a big man’s body, velvet-dark sapphires, chrysolite, beryl, amethyst, and the yellow zircon that is like transparent gold.

definitions: (chemistry) The oxide of zirconium, obtained as a white powder, and possessing both acid and basic properties. On account of its infusibility, and brilliant luminosity when incandescent, it is used as an ingredient of sticks for the Drummond light.
A chemical element (symbol Zr) with an atomic number of 40, a strong, lustrous, grey-white transition metal mainly used as a refractory and opacifier.
examples: Do you like how I dance? / I've got zirconium pants / Consequential enough / To slip you into a trance

definitions: (Canada, US, slang) Pimple
examples: I can't help thinking how little good all that working out did him. I think the only thing he ever got out of it was more zits.
Brad: Sara, did you take my Clearasil again? Sara: I ran out of brown (paint). Brad: Great. How am I supposed to cover up my zits?

definitions: (music) A musical instrument consisting of a flat sounding box with numerous strings placed on a horizontal surface, played with a plectrum or fingertips.

(music, translations) Related or similar instruments in other cultures, such as the Chinese guqin or Norwegian harpeleik; especially any chordophone without a neck, and with strings that pass over the body.
To make a sound similar to that made by a zither; to move while making such a sound.
To play a zither.
examples: The other men opened fire with pistols, slugs zithering past him with a dull whine.
‘Look at that!’ she shouted, already backing up the Jeep, its tyres zithering on the crusty surface.
The olives seemed weighed down under the weight of their fruit, smooth drops of green jade among which the choirs of cicadas zithered.

Once I owned a bike […] It had no fenders and one gear only, and on the forks, both fore and aft, I clipped some plastic playing cards to zither loudly through the spokes.

definitions: A zither
examples: The zitherns and mandolins have forms to match their pretty names, but the soul of the musical instrument abides elsewhere than in its body, and informs it only to the ear.

definitions: A type of macaroni pasta in the form of long smooth hollow tubes.
Any of several aquatic North American grasses, of the genus Zizania, grown for their edible grain; wild rice.
A buzzing or whizzing sound.
(informal) A nap or sleep.
examples: Then I had a little zizz until Martland telephoned.

definitions: (informal, intransitive) To take a nap.
The currency unit of Poland, divided into 100 groszy.
The belief that one has transformed into an animal.
A circle decorated with the signs of the zodiac.
(astronomy) The ecliptic: the belt-like region of the celestial sphere corresponding to the apparent path of the sun over the course of a year.
(astrology) The belt-like region of the celestial sphere, approximately eight degrees north and south of the ecliptic, which includes the apparent path of the sun, moon, and visible planets.
(nautical) A inflatable boat.
(astrology) The twelve equal divisions of the zodiacal region into signs or houses, each named for a prominent constellation in the region.
Any of various astrological systems considered similar to the above.
examples: So the Dendera Zodiac seems to be a complete copy of the Mesopotamian zodiac.

Tremulous on the bladed graſs ſhone bright the pearly drops, like an aſſembly of ſparkling ſtars dancing on the plains of the zodiac; vocal was every bending ſpray, every reed was inſpired, each vvarbling throat ſeemed to emulate the melodious voice of the bird of a thouſand ſongs.

definitions: (astronomy, astrology) Of or pertaining to the zodiac.
examples: She knew by heart everyone’s Zodiacal sign.
For several evenings the pale twinkling of the somber zodiacal stars lighted up the west side of the heavens for some time after the sun had set.
In the zodiacal procession of the poles, the 25,920-year cycle, Lascaux is the time of Virgo, and Virgo is associated with artistic excellence and discrimination.
[…] in some passages of the Old Testament they think they discover the Zodiacal course of the Sun […]

definitions: Having the form of an animal; animal-like, zoomorphic.
(grammar) referring to animals
Uncommon spelling of zombie.
examples: The spirit zombi, or the zombi of the ti bon ange alone, is carefully stored in a jar and may later be magically transmuted into insects, animals, or humans in order to accomplish the particular work of the bokor.

definitions: (figurative) Someone or something that should be dead but is not.
(computing) A process or task which has terminated but has not been removed from the list of processes, typically because it has an unresponsive parent process.
A cocktail of rum and fruit juices.
(figuratively) An apathetic or slow-witted person.
(Australia, slang) Marijuana, or similar drugs.
(philosophy) A hypothetical being that is indistinguishable from a normal human being except in that it lacks conscious experience, qualia, or sentience.
(figuratively) A human being in a state of extreme mental exhaustion.
(voodoo, horror) A person, usually undead, animated by unnatural forces (such as magic), with no soul or will of his or her own.
examples: The maitre d’ introduced us and I had a zombie with him. Those zombies are wicked.

The zombies were having fun The party had just begun The guests included Wolf-Man, Dracula, and his son.

After working for 18 hours on the computer, I was a zombie.

definitions: Any of species Bedotia madagascariensis
(medicine) shingles.
Certain freshwater fishes of genus Bedotia (Atheriniformes), from Madagascar:
Any of species Bedotia geayi
(sciences) A zone or band; a layer.
Divided into zones.
(of air or ocean currents) roughly longitudinal (east to west, or west to east)
Related to, associated with, or similar to zones.
examples: Decisions made at the DfT inevitably reflect a London-centric experience, with a comparison being made with the closure of ticket offices throughout the London Underground network. This is not judged to have been detrimental, but there is a simple zonal fares system that is hardly comparable with the range of ticket types used on the national rail network.

definitions: zonal
A given area distinguished on the basis of a particular characteristic, use, restriction, etc.
A restricted category or virtual place.
Any given region or area of the world.
The strike zone.
A belt or girdle.
A band or stripe extending around a body.
A circuit; a circumference.
That collection of a domain's DNS resource records, the domain and its subdomains, that are not delegated to another authority.
examples: Her tapered fingers too with rings are graced, / And an embroidered zone surrounds her slender waist.
I just got in the zone late in the game: everything was going in.
That pitch was low and away, just outside the zone.
The defender playing at the top of the zone is nine to fourteen metres out from the goal line.
There is a no-smoking zone that extends 25 feet outside of each entrance.

definitions: To assign to a restricted category.
To divide into or assign to sections or areas.
(intransitive, slang) To enter a daydream state temporarily, for instance as a result of boredom, fatigue, or intoxication; to doze off.
To define the property use classification of (an area).
examples: I must have zoned while he was giving us the directions.
This area was zoned for industrial use.
Please zone off our staging area, a section for each group.
We've zoned each other as friends; we'll never be anything more.

definitions: The use of zones, such as fare zones.
(law) Legislative action for the purpose of regulating the use of property and the construction of buildings within the area under the jurisdiction of the legislative body concerned.
examples: The system of zoning would lead logically to the introduction of the modern ticket barriers from 1987 (the Underground Ticketing System, or UTS), in that it allowed the software in the ticket gates to be relatively simple.

These towers are not only the product of advances in construction technology – and a global surfeit of super-rich buyers – but a zoning policy that allows a developer to acquire unused airspace nearby, add it to their own lot, and erect a vast structure without any kind of public review process taking place.

definitions: present participle and gerund of zone
(slang) The feeling of a drug taking hold.
(slang) An unfavorable card or token, or undesirable or worthless item used as a prize in a contest or game show (such as Let's Make a Deal).
(slang) A cigarette hand-rolled from tobacco and cannabis.
examples: Avoid the Zonk / On the TV show Let's Make a Deal, contestants often had to choose between three curtains.

There will always be two doors that hold zonks, so regardless of whether you initially chose the grand prize or a zonk, Monty will always be able to show you a zonk not chosen.

A live, mane-embellished, SAG-card-carrying lion, I should point out, who was likely being staged for a few minutes off to the side before he would be used as a freaking “ Zonk!” on a freaking game show, for crying out loud.

definitions: (transitive, slang) To hit hard.
(intransitive, slang, usually followed by “out”) To become exhausted, sleepy or delirious.
(transitive, slang) To make (someone) sleepy or delirious, to put into a stupor.
examples: After two hours of studying, I zonked out.

definitions: (anatomy) Any of several small belt -like regions.
examples: Nevertheless, the zonula is most often considered as the principal agent of transmission.

definitions: (anatomy) A little zone, or girdle; a zonula.
(anatomy) The zonule of Zinn.
examples: The rim of a small-sized lens along with zonule is clearly seen through the fully dilated pupil.

In most cases, the goal of this type of lens surgery is the same as that for eyes without a compromised zonule: to remove the contents of the capsular bag through a CCC [continuous curvilinear capsulectomy] and replace the contents with a foldable IOL. [intraocular lens]

definitions: (countable, US, military, slang) The jungle.
(countable, informal, figuratively) Any place that is wild, crowded, or chaotic.
A park where live animals are exhibited.
(countable, informal, figuratively) A large and varied collection of something.
(countable, informal) Clipping of zoophile.
examples: Zoophiles, or ' zoos,' are sexually and emotionally attracted to animals, as in a sexual orientation.

The shopping center was a zoo the week before Christmas.

But powerful as the quark model and the electroweak theory were in describing the zoo of subatomic particles, this still left a huge gap.

definitions: The practice of a human engaging in sexual intercourse with an animal
examples: A curious instance of perversion in religio-sexual feeling, bordering on zooerastia, is the case of St. Veronica.

definitions: The practice of a human engaging in sexual intercourse with an animal
(microbiology) Any of the heterotrophic flagellates of the phylum Zoomastigophora
(zoology) An animal in one of its inferior or early stages of development, as one of the intermediate forms in alternate generation.

(zoology) One of the individual animals in a composite group, as of Anthozoa, Hydrozoa, and Bryozoa; — sometimes restricted to those individuals in which the mouth and digestive organs are not developed.
(biology) An organic body or cell having locomotion, as a spermatic cell or spermatozoid.
(biology) Relating to, or resembling, an animal.
The worship of animals.
examples: Excessive zoolatry and heka practices had always been fundamental aspects of Egyptian religion and lifestyles, but from about the Late Period (c. 747 BC), they increasingly became tinged with fundamentalism and fanaticism.

definitions: Of, or relating to, zoology.
Of, or relating to, animals.
One who studies or practices zoology.
A treatise on this science.
The part of biology relating to the animal kingdom, including the structure, embryology, evolution, classification, habits, and distribution of all animals, both living and extinct.
A video teleconferencing call.
(figurative) A big increase.
(figurative) A quick ascent.
An augmentation of a view, by varying the focal length of a lens, or digitally.
A humming noise from something moving very fast.
examples: What's the zoom like on your camera?
Then, later that day, I logged onto a zoom call and my mother and I set up our yoga mats in the living room, as we had been doing a couple of times a week during the pandemic.
the zoom of traffic

definitions: To go up sharply.
To move fast with a humming noise.
To participate in a video teleconferencing call.
To check someone out; to investigate someone that one is interested in.
To manipulate a display so as to magnify or shrink it.
To move rapidly.
(photography) To change the focal length of a zoom lens.
(aviation) To zoom climb.
examples: We zoomed along the highway.
to zoom in or zoom out

"It makes you wonder if it's an insult to the intelligence of Essence ’s lesbian and gay leadership," Cunningham said. "Who's really zooming whom here?"

definitions: Suggesting something moving quickly
Suggesting a sudden change, especially an improvement or an increase
Representing a humming sound
examples: Makowsky was playing the Bassgeige. Zoom... zoom-zoom.... The rest of the orchestra would join in presently.
I would dance a few light fantastic steps to show which way the wind lay, and zoom! Like a breeze I was on the piano stool and doing a velocity exercise.

definitions: A zooflagellate: a heterotrophic flagellate of the phylum Zoomastigophora
The representation of gods as animals or the attributing of animal characteristics to gods.
The viewing of human behavior in terms of the behavior of animals.
(art, literature) The use of animal figures in art and design or of animal symbols in literature.
(biology, microbiology) An animal disease, such as rabies or anthrax, that can be transmitted to humans.
examples: In his first book since the 2008 essay collection Natural Acts: A Sidelong View of Science and Nature, David Quammen looks at the natural world from yet another angle: the search for the next human pandemic, what epidemiologists call “the next big one.” His quest leads him around the world to study a variety of suspect zoonoses —animal-hosted pathogens that infect humans.

definitions: relating to, or having a zoonosis
examples: Bats host many high-profile viruses that can infect humans, including severe acute respiratory syndrome and Ebola. A recent study explored the ecological variables that may contribute to bats’ propensity to harbor such zoonotic diseases by comparing them with another order of common reservoir hosts: rodents.

According to Prof Andre and many other experts, increased travel, the spread of mega cities and humanity’s gradual intrusion into more and more report areas means the chances of zoonotic “spillover” events like Covid are becoming more and more likely.

definitions: A paraphilia involving the sexual attraction of people to non-human animals.
The human sexual attraction or arousal to non-sapient animals.
(epidemiology) A preference of a parasite or vector to feed on non-human animals
An affection for non-human animals
Zoophilia, a sexual inclination towards bestiality
examples: Zoophilism bears witness to a new human being, one who no longer fears nature.

definitions: An unusual fear of animals.
examples: The girl who had zoophobia didn't like animals, domestic or wild.

definitions: (zoology) Free-floating small protozoa, crustaceans (such as krill), etc. and the eggs and larvae from larger animals.
Visual hallucinations in which a person perceives animals that are not actually present.
examples: One of her most persistent hallucinations was a small brightly patterned snake moving across the floor in the periphery of her vision. Her zoöpsia was accompanied by a terror of real animals.

definitions: A motile asexual spore of some algae and fungi
(furry fandom) A fursuit.
(slang, Trinidad and Tobago) A cigarette butt.
(slang, US) A zoot suit.
(slang, UK) A marijuana cigarette.
(uncountable, slang, UK) PCP; phencyclidine.

definitions: (intransitive, slang) To rush around quickly; to scoot.
examples: Lauren loves to zoot around the shopping mall. At top speed.
As well as allowing more and more people to zoot around the city in their cars, these roads also allowed for the improvement of public transport within Cardiff.

definitions: A toxin produced by an animal.
(especially Southeast US) A sandal, usually of rubber, secured to the foot by two straps mounted between the big toe and its neighbour.
A Japanese sandal made from rice straw or lacquered wood, worn with a kimono for formal occasions.
examples: He ran back and I watched him go, legs pumping, soles of his zori showing.

She took a while getting her crooked feet into her zori, but finally turned toward Mr. Tanaka and gave him a look he seemed to understand at once, because he left the room, closing the door behind him.

definitions: (uncountable, pathology) The disease called herpes zoster (from the typically beltlike pattern of its rash); shingles.
Any of a genus (Zoysia) of creeping perennial grasses of southern Asia and New Zealand having fine wiry leaves and including some suitable for lawn grasses, especially in warm regions.
examples: In areas farther north, zoysias will live but will not spread. Zoysias need bright, hot sunlight.

definitions: plural of zucchino

(international standards) Alternative letter-case form of Zulu from the NATO/ICAO Phonetic Alphabet.

examples: She nibbled the zwieback that she always fed off, And when it was gone, she cut the giant's head off.

definitions: A form of Louisiana Creole music, characteristically performed by accordion and washboard bands, that combines Cajun and Creole roots music with elements of African American music.
examples: Sunpie—in real life, a park ranger and an accordionist who leads a good zydeco band—taunted Harrison […].

definitions: Any cactus in the genus Schlumbergera (previously Zygocactus)
(ornithology) Any bird with toes of this kind, such as the parrot.
Having two toes pointing forward, and two pointing backward; zygodactylous.
examples: In parrots, the tarsometatarsus has a distinct shape resulting from the particular arrangement of parrot toes. As zygodactyl birds, toes number 2 and 3 are facing forward, while toes 1 and 4 are facing backwards.

definitions: (anatomy) The cheekbone.
(anatomy) Ellipsis of zygomatic bone.

definitions: (biology) Having bilateral symmetry.
zygomorphic
plural of zygomycete
(botany) A spore formed by the union of several zoospores.
(botany) A zygosperm.
(cytology, also attributive) A eukaryotic cell formed from the fusion of two gametes (“ reproductive cells ”) during a fertilization process. [from late 19th c.]
(biology) The intimate pairing of homologous chromosomes during the synaptic stage of meiosis
(biology) Of, pertaining to or existing as a zygote.
examples: zygotic divergence (analysis)

definitions: (biochemistry) Any of a group of enzymes that catalyze the fermentation of simple carbohydrates to ethanol and carbon dioxide.
examples: In 1905, Harden and Young found that the zymase of Buchner consisted of a thermolabile ferment fraction, which was later designated as "apo-zymase" by Neuberg and v. Euler, and of a thermostable ultrafiltrable factor, "co-zymase," the presence of which is necessary for the fermentation of sugar.

definitions: (biochemistry) A proenzyme, or enzyme precursor, which requires a biochemical change (i.e. hydrolysis) to become an active form of the enzyme.
A treatise on the use of fermentation to produce liquors.
The chemistry of fermentation with yeasts, especially the science involved in beermaking and winemaking; zymurgy.
(chemistry) The action of enzymes in breaking down organic substances during fermentation
Causing fermentation
A fermentation; hence, an analogous process by which an infectious disease is believed to be developed.
A zymotic disease.
Of or causing fermentation.
examples: Farr concluded that overcrowding was the main determinant of high mortality from what (following Liebig) he style ‘ zymotic diseases’.

The incidence of zymotic diseases upon any particular organ or set of organs, is, of course, well recognised; one recalls the nephritis of scarlatina, the endocarditis of rheumatism, gastritis of rabies, and—perhaps the most striking instance of the selective affinity of disease for specific local manifestation—the lesions of foot-and-mouth disease.

[…] these […] accounts by no means bear out the zymotic doctrine of the Board of Health as to the cause of cholera; for where the zymotic influences from the sewers were the worst, […] the cholera was the least destructive.

definitions: The practice of using fermentation to produce alcoholic beverages.
examples: Translated from Eis und Kaelte-industrie by an anonymous author.
The natural languages, however, cover every subject from aardvarks to zymurgies.]
Appears to be the earliest use of the word in print.]
Of Zymurgy I little know, / Perhaps because I little knead, / And can but make a sorry show / In singing liaisons of feed, / Flour, fodder,— […]

With great interest I learn from a pamphlet by Mr. Georg Barth, expert in zymurgy at Munich, which I have received lately, that the author, in his essay on "The Best Method of Storing Hops," arrives at the same conclusions which I have drawn in this article. Translated from Eis und Kaelte-industrie by an anonymous author.
